coredata.dc:title: Recent progress on passive cooling strategies for Li-ion battery of electric vehicles

coredata.dc:creator: Shruti, G.
Salunkhe, Pramod B.
Shenoy, B. Satish
coredata.dc:description: The battery is a vital component of an electric vehicle (EV), and an inadequate thermal management can lead to excessive temperature rise, non-uniform heating, and safety risks. This work presents a comprehensive evaluation of passive battery thermal management solutions for lithium-ion batteries under low to moderate operating conditions, with particular emphasis on phase change materials (PCMs) and PCM-heat pipe integration. Through critical review and comparative synthesis of recent experimental and modeling studies, the influence of pure, composite, and nano-enhanced phase change materials (NEPCMs), as well as different heat pipe configurations, is systematically assessed under varying discharge rates. Key performance indicators include maximum temperature, temperature uniformity (ΔT), system weight, and safety metrics such as flame retardancy. Results indicate that composite and NEPCMs can effectively maintain cell temperatures below 45 °C and ΔT < 5 °C at low to moderate discharge rates (typically 1C-3C); however, these thermal benefits are often accompanied by reductions in latent heat, shifts in melting temperature, and increased system weight. At higher C-rates, PCM-assisted heat pipe configurations demonstrate superior temperature control and improved thermal uniformity, though secondary cooling strategies may be required to ensure long-term reliability. The incorporation of flame-retardant PCM formulations is identified as critical for mitigating thermal runaway risks. By explicitly correlating passive cooling performance with discharge-rate-dependent thermal benchmarks, this review establishes practical selection guidelines for passive battery thermal management systems, offering actionable insights for improving EV battery pack reliability, lifespan, and safety.

row.entry: PCM + Air cooling and PCM + Liquid cooling
Hybrid BTMS integrating PCM and liquid/air cooling shows significant improvement in temperature control and uniformity under dynamic loading.

list-item.para: Most studies rely heavily on numerical simulations.
Real-world testing is needed to assess performance.
The durability of hybrid BTMS under practical conditions must be confirmed.

row.entry:
Effective PCMs with melting temperatures between 25 and 45 °C, enabling temporary heat absorption during peak loads.
Commonly used liquid coolants: water and water–ethylene-glycol (1:1); water–ethanol (3:2)
Air, Liquid, PCM, and Heat pipes
Poor thermal conductivity of PCM makes the system bulky and less efficient; spray cooling with nano-particle enhanced coolant was observed as the optimized cooling method.

list-item.para: Lack of integrated thermal models with real-time adaptability limits dynamic control under varying operating conditions.
Insufficient focus on high-discharge-rate scenarios common in heavy-duty electric vehicles.

row.entry:
Hybrid coolants are recommended for improved performance.
Hybrid coolant performance may be enhanced by integrating eco-friendly, non-toxic, and low-cost materials, including jute, wick filters, and hydrophilic fibers.
Pumping power increases with an increase in the percentage addition of nanoparticles. Carbon-based additives to the PCM have significant potential for battery cooling.

list-item.para: Lack of clarity on optimal nanoparticle concentration to balance thermal efficiency and pumping power.
Hybrid BTMS combining PCM and nanofluids is underexplored in real-world EV conditions.

row.entry: Carbon-additive PCMs enhance thermal uniformity and reduce battery temperatures, with expanded graphite–kaolin–paraffin composites achieving up to ∼28% reduction in maximum temperature at 4C

PCMs enhance LIB thermal control using latent heat for energy-efficient cooling without extra power. Active PCM systems offer better heat dissipation than passive ones in large-capacity battery packs.

list-item.para: More research is needed on integration with external and internal heat control systems.
Low thermal conductivity and integration challenges hinder widespread use in high-power LIB systems.
Insufficient attention to optimizing PCM thermophysical properties for practical, large-scale hybrid BTMS applications.

row.entry: PCM-based systems are more energy-efficient as they require no auxiliary power; however, their effectiveness is constrained by the narrow battery temperature sensitivity and phase-change range (∼30–50 °C).
PCM, Heat Pipes, Air cooling, Nano-fluid, Fins

Recent BTMS advancements for LIBs focus on climate-resilient heating/cooling, enhancing driving range, safety, lifespan, and charging efficiency, using innovations like PCMs, hybrid systems, and micro heat pipes.

list-item.para: Need for compact, lightweight, and cost-effective BTMS compatible with mass-market EVs.
Lack of research on predictive control using AI/ML.
Optimization of hybrid BTMS configurations remains unexplored
Real-time testing and established performance benchmarks are essential for rapid industrial adoption.

row.entry: Micro heat pipe arrays and pulsating heat pipes enhance low-temperature thermal performance, while air-cooled systems employing heat tubes with natural or forced airflow and flattened heat-pipe configurations demonstrate versatile cooling capability.

Passive and Hybrid BTMS based on PCM can prolong battery cycle life by 15–25% by keeping lithium-ion battery temperatures between 20 °C to 50 °C, ensuring good temperature uniformity and lowering peak temperatures by up to 20 °C.

list-item.para: Need for high-performance PCM composites with enhanced thermal conductivity, leakage resistance, and stability.
Limited optimization and modeling of hybrid BTMS for fast charging and extreme conditions.
Insufficient integration of AI-driven approaches for real-time thermal management.

row.entry:
Advancing PCM composite designs, such as graphene-enhanced and metal-foam-based systems, is essential to surpass current thermal conductivity limits of 5–50 W/m·K.
Optimizing cooling-channel design, working-fluid selection, and intermittent-cooling strategies can significantly enhance the efficiency of hybrid BTMSs.

Composite PCMs like aerogel-enhanced paraffin boost thermal conductivity by 150%, maintain safe battery temperatures, prevent capacity loss, while fins improve heat removal rapidly and maintain uniformity in PCM-based BTMS.

list-item.para: Limited testing under real-world cycling and high-rate charge-discharge conditions.
Insufficient optimization of fin geometry for specific battery configurations.
Limited research on PCM selection tailored to operating environments.

row.entry: Maintaining battery temperatures within 15–35 °C is recommended to maximize power output and energy efficiency, while effective thermal regulation is essential for preventing thermal runaway and ensuring safe operation; battery capacity can decrease by ∼50% at −30 °C and by ∼20% for every 10 °C drop in temperature.
Heat pipe, PCM, CPCM, liquid cooling, air cooling

Explored diverse HP geometries, advanced wick structures, and optimized working fluids. Hybrid systems combining HPs with air, liquid, PCMs, change materials, or thermoelectric elements deliver enhanced cooling performance and safety.

list-item.para: Lack of comprehensive comparative studies on wick structures, working fluids, and envelope materials suited for Li-ion battery temperature ranges.
Limited research on hybrid HP cooling configurations (e.g., PCM-HP,thermoelectric-HP) under real-time battery load conditions.

row.entry: AI techniques are used to optimize operating parameters, predict core temperatures in heat pipe systems, and apply advanced optimization and deep learning methods for efficient and safe management of heat-pipe-cooled reactors under strict power constraints.

At high discharge rates (≥4C), PCM–heat pipe cooling systems exhibit superior thermal performance, effectively limiting peak battery temperature and maintaining temperature uniformity (ΔT < 5 °C), thereby offering a reliable passive solution for high-power EV applications.

The composite and NEPCMs keep the maximum temperature < 45 °C and ΔT < 5 °C at (∼1C–3C); PCM–HP combined systems required at high discharge (≥4C).
Copper and aluminum heat pipes using water or ethanol are most suitable for battery operating temperature ranges.

head.ce:title: Recent progress on passive cooling strategies for Li-ion battery of electric vehicles

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Manipal Institute of Technology, Manipal Academy of Higher Education, Manipal, 576 104, Karnataka, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Mechanical Engineering, Manipal Institute of Technology, Manipal Academy of Higher Education, Manipal – 576 104, Karnataka, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The battery is a vital component of an electric vehicle (EV), and an inadequate thermal management can lead to excessive temperature rise, non-uniform heating, and safety risks. This work presents a comprehensive evaluation of passive battery thermal management solutions for lithium-ion batteries under low to moderate operating conditions, with particular emphasis on phase change materials (PCMs) and PCM-heat pipe integration. Through critical review and comparative synthesis of recent experimental and modeling studies, the influence of pure, composite, and nano-enhanced phase change materials (NEPCMs), as well as different heat pipe configurations, is systematically assessed under varying discharge rates. Key performance indicators include maximum temperature, temperature uniformity (ΔT), system weight, and safety metrics such as flame retardancy. Results indicate that composite and NEPCMs can effectively maintain cell temperatures below 45 °C and ΔT < 5 °C at low to moderate discharge rates (typically 1C-3C); however, these thermal benefits are often accompanied by reductions in latent heat, shifts in melting temperature, and increased system weight. At higher C-rates, PCM-assisted heat pipe configurations demonstrate superior temperature control and improved thermal uniformity, though secondary cooling strategies may be required to ensure long-term reliability. The incorporation of flame-retardant PCM formulations is identified as critical for mitigating thermal runaway risks. By explicitly correlating passive cooling performance with discharge-rate-dependent thermal benchmarks, this review establishes practical selection guidelines for passive battery thermal management systems, offering actionable insights for improving EV battery pack reliability, lifespan, and safety.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: With increasing population and industrialization, the energy demand has been increased significantly. Currently, fossil fuels account for more than 80% of total worldwide power consumption. It has become vital for renewable energy to replace the fossil fuels as the primary source of electricity due to the significant growth in environmental contamination and the depletion of conventional fossil fuels [1–3]. The automotive industry is passing through a transition from internal combustion engines to electric and hybrid vehicles. The primary reason for the adoption of electric vehicles (EVs) is to prevent the generation of greenhouse gases such as nitrous oxides, carbon dioxide, methane, and fluorinated gases.

Several countries have made significant investments in the renewable energy sector to meet the growing demand for electricity. According to the International Renewable Energy Agency, the global renewable energy capacity is anticipated to expand by over 5520 GW from 2024 to 2030, surpassing the deployment achieved in the previous six years (2017–2023) by 2.6 times [4,5]. It is recognized that the transition to electric mobility will help minimize greenhouse gas emissions and lower the strain on the environment [6,7]. It was reported that EVs can reduce CO2 emission by an average of 1.5 million grams [8]. Guo et al. [9] established a life cycle carbon emission model which indicates about 47% reduction in carbon emission for the EVs as compared to the gasoline vehicles. This demonstrates the role of EVs in energy conservation and emission reduction. Fig. 1 shows annual emission for different types of vehicles. It clearly depicts a sharp rise in the emission level for the gasoline engines.

The current global warming situation is at an alarming level and researchers are trying their best to reduce carbon emission. The Mckinsey analysis predicts that the EV market will explode by 2030, with sales in the US rising by 36%, in China by 52%, and in Europe by 44%. In India, it is anticipated that 30% of automobiles will be electrified by the year 2030 [11]. Today's EVs are more sophisticated and safer than the earlier models. Supportive government policies and infrastructure development have resulted in a significant increase in EV production and adoption [12,13].

In the near future, EVs may ideally continue to advance as more economical transportation. Over the past ten years, electric vehicles have gained significant global adoption. It's impressive to see the continued growth in global EV sales aligning with expectations set at the beginning of 2023. A total sale of 14.2 million new Battery electric vehicles (BEVs) and Plug-in hybrid vehicles (PHEVs) during 2023 represents strong momentum in electrification [14]. The global BEV and PHEV sales are shown in Fig. 2 .

EVs have several benefits, namely, pollution-free transportation [15–17], long term cost-effectiveness [18,19], significant reduction in respiratory health related issues [20–22], improved energy security [23,24], technological advancement [25–27] etc. Despite many benefits of EVs, there are a few challenges, such as lack of charging infrastructure [28,29], longer charging time [30], higher battery cost and limited lifespan of the battery [31,32], requirement of battery thermal management system [33,34] etc. These issues are crucial. The EV battery works efficiently in the operational temperature range of 20–40 °C. At high charge/discharge rates, the chemical reactions within the battery pack can generate substantial heat and create hotspots.

The EV battery community may have to address the above-mentioned challenges to accelerate the adoption of EVs and make them a more viable alternative to the traditional vehicles. Promising strategies to further enhance EV adoption include,
•
Increasing the EV range: EV manufacturers can continue to improve battery technology, to increase driving range.
•
Expansion of the charging infrastructure: Governments and private companies can invest in expanding the charging infrastructure to make it more accessible to the consumers. The development of fast-charging stations may reduce the charging time, especially on long trips [35]. The accessibility to a large number of public charging stations would significantly benefit the customers and subsequently may help increase the EV sales [36].
•
Reducing battery cost and increasing lifespan: According to the Goldman Sachs Research, the advancements in battery technology to boost the energy density, coupled with a decline in green metal prices, are expected to drive battery costs lower than the earlier projections [37]. The battery cost can be reduced through research and development on new materials and techniques. The battery lifespan can be substantially extended by incorporating a suitable battery thermal management system [38].
•
The battery thermal management system is crucial because thermal issues strongly affect performance and lifespan. As a result, an effective BTMS is required to maintain a safe temperature range within the battery pack. Two broad categories of BTMS for the EVs are internal and external BTMS [39,40]. Fig. 3 shows a detailed classification of the BTMS.

The battery temperature rises due to several factors, including high discharge rates, rapid charging, environmental conditions, internal resistance of the battery etc. Thermal runaway may occur if the heat generation exceeds the heat dissipation capacity of the battery, resulting in an accelerating and self-sustaining cycle of rising temperatures. As the temperature rises, reaction rates increase, which produces more heat and subsequently, it may lead to the breakdown of the battery. This can release gases, increase internal pressure, and ultimately result in uncontrollable overheating, fire, or even explosion, if not effectively managed. By regulating the temperature within an optimal range, BTMS enhances battery efficiency and prevents dangerous conditions like thermal runaway. In addition to limiting peak temperature, efficient thermal management in lithium-ion batteries also slows down the electrochemical breakdown process. Elevated temperatures accelerate solid–electrolyte interphase (SEI) growth, transition-metal dissolution, and electrolyte oxidation, which increase impedance and capacity fade, especially under high-rate cycling. By maintaining moderate and evenly distributed temperatures, advanced cooling strategies mitigate these reactions, thereby extending cycle life and enhancing safety. BTMS also adapts to extreme environmental conditions, ensuring reliable performance in both hot and cold climates. It minimizes energy loss, improves charge and discharge rates, and lowers maintenance and replacement costs, contributing to the overall system efficiency and sustainability.

The BTMS is primarily classified into three categories, namely active, passive, and hybrid cooling systems. The active cooling system removes heat from a battery pack by circulating a coolant (air, gas, or liquid) through it. Air cooling is one of the most simple, easy, and widely used cooling techniques for the battery packs [41]. However, due to the lower specific heat of the air, it is limited to the low-power batteries only. In contrast, liquid cooling is quite popular, especially for high-power batteries, due to its large specific heat and higher thermal conductivity than air. Liquid cooling may be of a direct or indirect type. In the former, the battery pack is completely immersed in the liquid coolant, usually the dielectric fluid. In the latter, there is an indirect contact between the heat transfer fluid and the battery cell. The heat transfer fluid is circulated through the channels/jackets, which are in direct contact with the battery cells. Automotive industries have different approaches to cooling the battery pack. The commercial implementation of BTMS in electric vehicles has significantly advanced in recent years. Leading EV manufacturers, including Tesla, BYD, Nissan etc., have implemented advanced liquid-based cooling systems to ensure temperature uniformity, fast charging, and improved safety during high C-rate operation [42,43]. BYD's blade battery employs a cell-to-pack architecture with long prismatic Lithium‑iron-phosphate cells that inherently reduce heat generation and enhance heat dissipation. This enables effective thermal control through simplified passive cooling strategies rather than the complex active BTMS. The blade battery allows cells to be placed directly in contact with pack-level cooling plates, improving heat transfer pathways and reducing thermal resistance between the cell and cooling system [44].Recent system-level studies on commercial EVs have demonstrated that battery thermal management architecture can change driving-range losses by more than 20% between models such as Tesla Model 3, Chevrolet Bolt, and Nissan Leaf, especially under extreme hot or cold conditions, underscoring the need for more efficient BTMS solutions [45]. The Tesla Model S employs a dielectric-coated aluminum cooling tube that circulates liquid coolant between cylindrical Li-ion cells, using a counter-flow design to enhance heat dissipation and temperature uniformity across the battery pack [46]. To increase the cooling capacity, the combination of active liquid cooling and passive cooling may be beneficial [47]. Battery thermal management is closely linked to broader electric vehicle challenges, particularly the deployment of fast-charging infrastructure. High charging rates substantially increase heat generation, making effective thermal regulation essential for maintaining safety, performance, and battery lifespan. Integrating passive and hybrid thermal management systems with battery management systems (BMS) enables coordinated electro-thermal control, where real-time temperature feedback can be used to regulate charging current, cell balancing, and operational limits. Such system-level integration enhances fast-charging reliability and supports scalable, energy-efficient EV operation.

Passive cooling refers to battery cooling without the use of any external energy source. It may be accomplished by using the phase change materials (PCMs) [48,49], fins [50,51], heat pipes [52,53] etc. A major drawback of the PCM is their poor thermal conductivity [54,55]. It is compensated by different techniques, namely, the addition of high thermal conductivity enhancers such as expanded Graphite [56,57] and Graphene [58–60], addition of metal matrix [61,62], addition of nanoparticles [63,64] etc. To provide a holistic understanding of recent advancements, the past literature has been synthesized into comprehensive visual summaries. Fig. 4 highlights the chronological evolution of passive BTMS. The conventional PCM cooling is followed by composite PCM cooling, NEPCM and fin cooling, microencapsulated and hybrid PCM-heat pipe cooling and most recently the AI-assisted hybrid cooling.

The passive cooling techniques have the potential to control the battery pack temperature and its potential hazards to keep the battery operation safe and improve its lifespan. Table 1 shows an overview of key findings and lacunae of review articles in passive/hybrid BTMS.

Although prior reviews highlight the effectiveness of passive PCM-based thermal management systems, most of them emphasize material-level conductivity enhancement or peak temperature reduction without clearly correlating performance with operational benchmarks such as discharge rate and temperature uniformity. As a result, the applicability limits of passive cooling strategies remain insufficiently defined. Despite extensive research on passive battery thermal management techniques, achieving simultaneous uniform temperature distribution and efficient heat dissipation in lithium-ion battery packs during high-rate discharge remains a critical challenge. Conventional air cooling methods often fail to maintain temperature uniformity within safe limits (ΔT < 5 °C), especially at high discharge rates. Although PCMs and heat pipes individually offer latent heat storage and rapid heat transport capabilities, their optimal integration and the role of thermal conductivity enhancement strategies, such as nano-enhancement, microencapsulation, and hybrid fin and heat sink designs, are not comprehensively understood. This study addresses these gaps by systematically investigating and comparing various passive cooling technologies and conductivity enhancement methods to identify effective solutions for maintaining thermal safety and performance in battery packs under demanding operational conditions.

The primary aim of the present paper is to unravel the influence of different passive cooling techniques, such as PCM and heat pipes, on the thermal management of a battery pack. Additionally, it also explores the role of various thermal conductivity enhancement strategies for the PCM, namely, composite PCM, NEPCM, micro-encapsulated PCM, and fins and heat sinks, in controlling the battery pack temperature and maintaining the uniform temperature distribution within it. Such in-depth studies on passive thermal management are limited in the past literature, which covers all types of passive cooling techniques, including the thermal conductivity enhancement strategies.

The refereed journal articles were collected in battery thermal management domain, mostly in the past ten years, and critically analyzed and summarized in the present work. The paper is organized as follows: Section 2 deals with the phase change material and various thermal conductivity enhancement strategies. Section 3 highlights the working principle of the heat pipe and its influence on the battery thermal management system. It is followed by the conclusions and future scope for the research.

Phase change materials are substances that can store and release large amounts of thermal energy during a phase change process, mostly the solid-liquid phase transformation and vice-versa [73]. The thermophysical properties of the PCMs, such as high latent heat of fusion, high specific heat, appropriate melting point temperature, etc., make them useful in various applications, including thermal energy storage systems [74,75], building conditioning [76,77], temperature regulation in electronics [78], food preservation [79,80] to name a few.

A broad classification of the PCMs is shown in Fig. 5 . PCMs are broadly classified into organic, inorganic, and eutectic materials, each exhibiting distinct thermal transport characteristics. Organic PCMs, such as paraffins and non-paraffins, offer high latent heat and chemical stability but suffer from inherently low thermal conductivity, which limits heat dissipation during high-rate battery operation. Inorganic PCMs, including salt hydrates and metallic alloys, provide higher thermal conductivity but often face challenges related to subcooling, phase segregation, and corrosion. To overcome the low thermal conductivity of organic PCMs, composite and NEPCMs are developed by dispersing high-conductivity fillers or nanoparticles, which form conductive heat transfer pathways within the PCM matrix and enhance effective thermal conductivity [81,82]. Organic PCMs are commonly used in BTMS applications as they are easily available, cost-effective and have low subcooling and better chemical stability. Over the past few decades, numerous investigations on PCMs have been carried out [83,84].

Zhang et al. [85] recommended focusing on PCMs that can provide higher structural strength and flame-retardant properties rather than the thermal conductivity enhancers that may reduce the latent heat of the composite PCMs. Along with the PCM cooling, it was suggested to use either an active (forced convection cooling) or passive (heat pipes) cooling system to improve the cooling effect. Similarly, Zichen et al. [86] also recommended the use of a PCM-based hybrid cooling system for improved thermal performance. However, the importance of incorporating the thermal conductivity enhancer in PCM has been highlighted. Parhizi and Jain [87] reported that the cell thermal conductivity is equally important as the PCM thermal conductivity. The artificial increment in PCM thermal conductivity with the addition of additives may reduce the cell surface temperature. However, as the cell thermal conductivity is poor, the core temperature of the cell remains relatively unaffected even after the increase in PCM thermal conductivity. The same happens with the latent heat of the PCM. Therefore, it was recommended to improve the cell thermal conductivity along with the PCM thermal conductivity for better cooling effects.

Togun et al. [88] investigated various cooling techniques: air, liquid, PCM, heat pipe, refrigerant, and hybrid cooling and highlighted the limitations of traditional air and indirect liquid cooling methods. The growing importance of direct liquid cooling and hybrid cooling strategies integrating advanced materials like nanofluids and NEPCMs was emphasized. The critical challenges were addressed in achieving temperature uniformity, energy efficiency, system compactness, and safety. It was reported that the modern BTMS designs are increasingly leveraging intelligent algorithms and control methods, such as machine learning and evolutionary optimization, to improve temperature regulation and prevent thermal runaway. Zhi et al. [89] reviewed the use of organic PCMs for the BTMS application. The organic PCMs are inherently combustible in nature and, therefore, in case of thermal runaway, these PCMs cannot prevent the occurrence of thermal runaway. It is, therefore, advised to focus on the flame-retardant properties and shape stability to enable widespread commercial application of the PCMs for battery cooling applications.

To increase thermal conductivity, numerous researchers have combined PCM with various thermal conductivity enhancers such as expanded graphite (EG), graphene, metal matrix, fins, etc. The following sections carry out a detailed survey on various kinds of thermal conductivity enhancement strategies for the PCM.

A composite PCM (CPCM) is a mixture of PCM and high thermal conductivity materials, such as expanded graphite, graphene etc. Though the addition of these thermal conductivity enhancers substantially improves the thermal conductivity of the PCM, it negatively influences the latent heat of the PCM. Therefore, these enhancers should be added with limited proportion only. The effective thermal conductivity of composite and NEPCMs is commonly predicted using effective medium and mixing models. Classical models such as Maxwell–Garnett and Bruggeman theories are widely applied at low to moderate filler concentrations [90,91], while Hamilton–Crosser formulations [92] account for the influence of non-spherical, high-aspect-ratio fillers such as expanded graphite and graphene. At higher filler loadings, percolation-based models [93] are employed to capture the formation of conductive networks. For NEPCMs, models incorporating interfacial thermal resistance provide improved prediction accuracy. However, deviations from theoretical models are frequently observed due to particle agglomeration, suppressed natural convection, and latent heat degradation.

Oya et al. [94] added expanded graphite to erythritol (C4H8O4), significantly enhancing its thermal conductivity by forming conductive percolation clusters due to the graphite's high aspect ratio and complex structure. The improved thermal conductivity, up to 6.4 times that of pure erythritol, closely matched theoretical predictions, highlighting the crucial role of filler shape and concentration. However, challenges remained in achieving uniform dispersion and optimizing percolation without sacrificing mechanical integrity, indicating areas for future research to optimize these composites for practical thermal energy storage applications. Mei et al. [95] combined the paraffin with EG and fins for the thermal management of the battery pack. The combination of PCM with EG performed better than the PCM with fins. The best performance was achieved with the combination of PCM with EG and fins, resulting in a maximum reduction in battery pack temperature by 35.5%. The PCM-based cooling system was observed to fail at a high ambient temperature of 60 °C, however, it could control the battery pack temperature after combining EG and fins with the PCM. Goli et al. [96] added 20 wt% graphene to the paraffin and observed a significant rise in thermal conductivity from 0.25 to 45 W/m.K. Without PCM, the battery pack experienced the highest temperature rise of 37 °C. After the incorporation of PCM, the temperature rise reduced to 24 °C, which was further reduced to 10 °C after using the hybrid graphene-PCM mixture. Both experimental and simulation investigations have consistently demonstrated graphene's outstanding thermal conductivity and strong interfacial compatibility with organic matrices, leading to substantial enhancements in overall heat transfer performance. However, persistent challenges related to achieving homogeneous graphene dispersion and maintaining long-term thermal and structural stability remain unresolved, underscoring the need for further systematic optimization to enable scalable and durable practical applications. Cao et al. [97] combined the CPCM (paraffin and EG) with nano-PCM emulsion (10 wt% paraffin, 2 wt% surfactant, and 88 wt% water) to improve the Li-ion battery thermal performance. The numerical simulations showed that this combination outperforms the water/CPCM cooling system, achieving better temperature control with lower power consumption. The system maintains battery temperatures below 48 °C and temperature difference under 4 °C over multiple charge–discharge cycles.

Metal foam plays a key role in improving the heat transfer rate of an EV battery. A few of the commonly used metal foams are copper, aluminum, nickel foams, etc. The combination of PCM and the porous metal foam may be significantly advantageous for the cooling of the battery packs. The PCM resides within the porous structure of the foam and does not spill out even during the phase change process. Wang et al. [98] impregnated the paraffin inside the copper foam to promote the heat transfer rate. The study demonstrated that integrating copper foam and paraffin significantly enhances heat transfer, reducing the heat storage time by approximately 40% due to the high thermal conductivity of the foam. Zou et al. [99] added multi-walled carbon nanotubes (MWCNT) and graphene in a mass ratio of 3/7 to the PCM for the battery pack cooling application. The thermal conductivity of the composite PCM was significantly increased by 31.8%, 55.4%, and 124% as compared to the graphene-PCM mixture, MWCNT-PCM mixture, and pure PCM, respectively. The best composite PCM configuration substantially controlled the battery pack temperature even after the completion of the phase change process. Huang et al. [100] prepared a flexible form-stable CPCM consisting of PCM, flexible polymer, and EG in a 80:20:3 proportion. Two PCMs were used, namely, eicosane (CPCM-1) and tetracosane (CPCM-2). The maximum temperature drop for the battery pack was 6.5 °C and 20 °C for CPCM-1 and CPCM-2, respectively. In subsequent studies, Huang et al. [101] used paraffin/EG CPCM and reported that thermal conductivity and latent heat play a major role in the heat transfer process up to only a certain extent. Beyond the optimal thermal conductivity, the improvement in heat transfer diminishes due to reduction in latent heat storage and deterioration in mechanical stability of the composite PCM. The optimum value of latent heat was found to be 225 J/g. The combination of paraffin with metal foam led to the improved heat transfer rate, keeping the maximum temperature difference within the pack lower than 1 °C. Darkwa et al. [102] developed a laminated CPCM composed of aluminum particles and hexadecane with a polyvinyl acetate as an appropriate adhesive material for bonding the components. Results indicated that the thermal conductivity improved from 0.15 to 1.25 W/m.K, and heat storage capacity was reduced by 25% as compared to the pure hexadecane. The CPCM improved the heat transfer rate by 10% and 15% during charging and discharging, respectively. It was recommended to explore other binding materials that may have a slight influence on the thermophysical properties of the CPCM.

Igweh et al. [103] introduced an innovative PCM-based BTMS incorporating air pockets within the PCM jacket to improve heat regulation and reduce overall mass. The air pockets function as localized thermal resistance zones, moderating heat conduction so that the PCM melts more gradually, prolonging its liquid phase and maintaining stable temperatures. Evaluating air pocket thicknesses of 0.5–1.7 mm, the optimal design with a 1.4 mm air pocket in conserved PCM volume achieved the best performance by balancing insulation and latent heat storage, extending liquid-phase stability by 14% and enhancing long-term cooling efficiency with minimal mass increase. Meanwhile, a 0.5 mm air pocket in a non-conserved PCM jacket provided the optimal trade-off between weight reduction and efficient temperature control, as thicker pockets added excessive insulation that hindered heat transfer. Huang et al. [104] synthesized core-shell structured microcapsules through in-situ polymerization, using paraffin as the core and melamine-formaldehyde as the shell. Methanol was added to the shell material to enhance the thermal stability and reduce toxicity. Composites were formed by adding three different types of heat-conductive fillers to the microcapsules: carbon nanotubes, nano-Al2O3 (aluminum oxide), and nano-ZnO (Zinc oxide). The sample containing 10% carbon nanotubes had the highest latent heat of 139.64 J/g and the highest thermal conductivity of 0.50 W/m.K. Tests for leakage and battery charge/discharge demonstrated that the addition of carbon nanotubes greatly improved the battery's latent heat storage capacity and dissipation performance, lowering its surface temperature by 12% as compared to the Al2O3 and ZnO. Septiadi et al. [105] added 20% mass fraction of EG to the PCM, which increased the thermal conductivity by 104 times. The system was evaluated under 0.5C, 1C, and 1.5C discharge rates, simulating real driving conditions with an actual e-bike. The BTMS effectively reduced the battery temperature to 37 °C at 1.5C discharge rate. Critically, the results highlighted an effective balance between thermal conductivity and latent heat capacity in the CPCM, which is essential for maintaining thermal regulation without compromising the stability of the PCM. Table 2 provides an overview of significant findings of CPCM in heat dissipation from the EV batteries.

Although the CPCM studies summarized in Table 2 are predominantly supported by experimental validation using battery modules and embedded temperature measurements, most investigations are conducted under constant C-rate discharge conditions and controlled ambient environments. While such steady-state experiments are essential for isolating material-level thermal behavior and benchmarking peak temperature reduction, they do not fully replicate the highly transient current profiles encountered in real-world EV operations. Consequently, validation of CPCM-based and hybrid thermal management systems under dynamic driving cycles, involving rapid load fluctuations and repeated acceleration–braking events, remains limited in the literature. Numerical simulations are therefore frequently employed to study transient thermal responses, highlighting the need for future experimental studies that combine embedded sensing with drive-cycle-based current profiles.

Many studies have experimentally investigated composite PCM–based battery modules using embedded thermocouples, demonstrating effective temperature control across multiple discharge rates and improved temperature uniformity in cylindrical lithium-ion battery modules through spatially distributed sensor measurements [116–118]. Beyond static performance indicators, recent experimental studies validate hybrid energy storage and powertrain systems under standardized transient driving cycles such as worldwide harmonized light vehicles test procedure (WLTP), worldwide harmonized light vehicles test cycles (WLTC), and China light-duty vehicle test cycle part (CLTC-P) using chassis dynamometers or hardware-in-the-loop test benches [119,120]. These validation protocols integrate calibrated vehicle–powertrain–control models with high-resolution measurements of DC-bus voltage, source currents, state of charge (SOC), and temperature, enabling assessment of both electrical and thermal responses under rapidly varying loads. Such cycle-resolved validation frameworks demonstrate the ability of hybrid systems to maintain stable power distribution and thermal regulation under realistic operating conditions, and they provide a robust benchmark for assessing the transient performance of battery thermal management strategies beyond constant C-rate testing.

Wang et al. [121] analyzed the effect of thickness of silicone and PCM for BTMS. The silicone layer was varied to 1, 2, and 3 mm, whereas, PCM layer to 10, 12, and 14 mm. The optimal configuration was obtained with 14 mm thick PCM and 3 mm thick silicone layer which reduced the battery module temperature by 24 °C at 4C discharge rate as compared to the PCM alone. The silica gel layer over the PCM surface, prevents the PCM leakage, and improves the mechanical properties of the composite PCM, leading to the enhanced cooling performance However, the study also revealed that beyond certain thickness and thermal conductivity thresholds, the cooling benefits levelled off, as the resistance to the heat transfer shifted to interfacial and convective regions, thereby limiting further performance gains. Lin et al. [122] added expanded graphite matrix and graphite sheets to the PCM to control the battery temperature. For the discharge current of 40 A and 80 A, the maximum reduction in battery temperature was 32% and 37%, respectively. Up to a 2C discharge rate, the maximum temperature difference within the battery module was substantially lower than 5 °C. Critically, while the passive BTMS excels for short-distance and moderate use, it may be insufficient for high-load and long-distance electric vehicle operation, necessitating hybrid systems for continuous thermal regulation. Thus, this study provided a practical, energy-efficient solution yet acknowledges the need for further research into scalability and integration with other cooling techniques for broader EV applications.

Xu et al. [123] conducted a detailed parametric and simulation-based analysis of a hybrid battery thermal management system incorporating ultrathin PCM layers between liquid cooling channels and prismatic lithium-ion batteries. The PCM layers reduce sensitivity to coolant velocity due to their latent heat buffering effect, enabling efficient operation at low velocity (0.05 m/s) with minimal energy use. The optimal PCM thickness (0.2 mm) achieved the best balance between temperature rise control and uniformity (ΔT < 5 °C). Increasing the channel aspect ratio (8:1) reduced maximum temperature, though slightly compromising uniformity, whereas a smaller ratio (4:2) improves thermal uniformity across the pack. The aligned channel configuration with uniform spacing and alternating flow delivers the most even temperature profile by minimizing flow recirculation.

According to the International Organization for Standardization (ISO), the nano-particles (NPs) are defined as the nano-objects having all exterior dimensions in the nano-scale, where the lengths of the longest and the shortest axes do not appreciably differ from one another [124,125]. NPs come in a variety of forms, dimensions, and geometries. They may have shapes such as spherical, cylindrical, conical, tubular, hollow core, spiral, etc. NPs are often classified into three classes based on their constitution: organic, carbon-based, and inorganic [126]. Fig. 6 shows the classification of nano-particles. Nano-particles can be mixed with the phase change material in different weight proportions to enhance their thermal conductivity and the stability [127,128].

There are two approaches to incorporate nano-particles into the PCM, one as a dispersed phase and another one as a coating [129]. In the dispersed phase method, nano-particles are uniformly mixed into PCM. This method can enhance the thermal conductivity of the PCM, which may help improve the heat transfer rate. However, this method may reduce the melting point of the nano-PCM. In the coating method, the nano-particles are deposited on the surface of the PCM to increase its surface area and stability. This method also increases the thermal conductivity and reduces the melting point of the nano-PCM [130,131]. Numerous researchers have investigated the impact of incorporating nano-particles into PCM. Yang et al. [132] analyzed hybrid BTMS that integrate nanofluids and nano-PCMs with conventional active (air and liquid cooled) and passive cooling (PCM) approaches, demonstrating clear advantages in enhancing heat dissipation, thermal uniformity, and the safety of lithium-ion batteries across various applications. However, it was reported that the performance of BTMS at different C-rates and operating conditions cannot be easily compared due to methodological variations, and that further optimization, potentially through advanced algorithms and comprehensive economic analysis, is required. Kumar et al. [133] reviewed the role of nanoparticles in BTMS applications and reported that the addition of nano-additives such as carbon nanotubes and metal oxides significantly enhances the thermal management of lithium-ion batteries by improving the thermal conductivity, heat capacity, and stability of PCMs. These enhancements enable more efficient heat absorption and dissipation, resulting in improved temperature control and extended battery life, especially at high charge/discharge rates. Further, it was recommended to investigate the effect of nanoparticles on the latent heat of composite PCMs. Bahiraei et al. [134] formulated paraffin-based nano-composites with various carbon-based NPs, namely carbon nano-fiber, graphene NPs, and graphite nano-powder with varying mass fractions of 2.5 to 10%. The graphite nano-powder with 10% mass fraction led to the highest increase in thermal conductivity by 11 times. The addition of NPs substantially improves the viscosity of the nano-PCM which hinders the natural convection heat transfer for the liquid PCM. This shows a trade-off in using nano-PCMs between the significant increase in thermal conductivity and deterioration in natural convection heat transfer. Karimi et al. [135] investigated the thermal performance of a battery-like simulator using various PCM composites. Among the tested nano-composites, silver nano-PCM exhibited the lowest battery surface temperature. The metal matrix provides a stable, highly conductive thermal pathway throughout the composite, promoting uniform heat distribution and minimizing temperature gradients within the cell. This uniformity translated to nearly 70% reduction in temperature differences across the battery, enhancing thermal stability and preventing localized hot spots that could degrade battery life or safety. Taghilou et al. [136] introduced CuO nano-particles in the range of 1 to 5 wt% to the PCM, n-Octadecane. The increase in ambient temperature from −20 °C to 50 °C raised the battery temperature by 17% without PCM and by 1.7% with PCM. The addition of copper oxide NPs decreased the battery temperature by 1 °C and improved the liquid fraction by 15.2%. While the increased thermal conductivity facilitates a higher heat transfer rate, the incorporation of NPs lowers the PCM's latent heat capacity, thereby diminishing its ability to absorb and store thermal energy during phase transition. As a result, despite the rapid heat conduction, the overall cooling effect is limited, leading to only a slight decrease in battery temperature.

Alqaed et al. [137] added graphene NPs to the PCM, and performed numerical simulations on melting-freezing cycles of the nano-PCM for the cooling of a triangular lithium-ion battery pack. The battery pack was located at the center of a circular enclosure with 12 blades attached to the sides of the battery. The impact of blade length on heat transfer, temperature distribution, and phase change efficiency is analyzed. It was observed that the nano-PCM melts faster than it freezes, with longer blades accelerating both processes and improving thermal regulation. It is attributed to the enhanced heat transfer due to natural convection during the liquid phase. While the bladeless enclosure slows the phase change process. Babapoor et al. [138] added carbon fiber to a PCM to increase the battery cooling efficiency. A mixture of PCM with 2 mm long carbon fibers of mass percentage of 0.46% demonstrated the highest thermal performance, enabling a 45% reduction in the maximum temperature of the battery. Even at low concentrations, carbon fibers form thermal pathways within the PCM, significantly improving the heat dissipation. Their elongated structure (2 mm length) promotes anisotropic conduction, allowing faster heat transfer from the cell. This enhances the PCM's ability to absorb and distribute heat more uniformly, preventing local hotspots and resulting in a substantial temperature drop. Table 3 shows a summary of nano-particles incorporated with different PCMs.

Recent studies have introduced innovative hybrid system designs that significantly enhance the thermal management efficiency beyond earlier approaches, addressing key limitations such as thermal conductivity, scalability, and integration with EV battery packs. Wang et al. [139] developed an integrated BTMS combining magnetic-field-assisted nano-enhanced phase change material (NEPCM) cooling with electrothermal film preheating, enabling dual control for high- and low-temperature environments. The optimized configuration using 8 mm thick NEPCM with 6 wt% Fe₃O₄ nanoparticles reduced the maximum temperature rise by 29.3% under 3C discharge at 35 °C and maintained exceptional temperature uniformity (<2.2 °C). The application of 900 Gs axial magnetic field promoted symmetric phase change, improving heat dissipation and avoiding local hot spots compared to radial fields. The axial magnetic field promotes uniform magnetic buoyancy forces around the NEPCM layer, causing symmetrical melting and fluid circulation, which improved temperature uniformity and prevented hot-spot formation. In contrast, radial fields generated uneven buoyancy directions, resulting in asymmetric melting and localized thermal resistance.

Park et al. [156] synthesized PCM nano-capsules with magnetite (Fe3O4) nano-particles implanted based on a poly-urea shell and paraffin core. The thermal conductivity of the nano-PCM improved from 0.22 to 9.7 W/m.K. This enhancement facilitates efficient heat conduction, reducing the supercooling effect by promoting uniform nucleation during phase change. However, it decreases the latent heat of the PCM. It is attributed to the reduction in PCM content due to the incorporation of nanoparticles. Ranjan et al. [157] added 2 wt% and 4 wt% Al2O3 to the paraffin wax to cool the Li-ion battery. The highest reduction in battery temperature was 23% and 27% for the 2 wt% and 4 wt% nano-PCMs, respectively. For the former, the maximum temperature difference within a cell was higher than 5 °C for a discharge rate of 1.38C. Therefore, it was recommended to use air cooling along with the nano-PCM to keep the temperature difference below 5 °C. Subsequently, Bais et al. [158] analyzed the effect of γ-Alumina nano-particles in different proportions of 0.5, 1, 1.5, 2, and 4 wt% to the PCM, RT-47. The experiments were conducted at a high discharge rate of 3C. The best thermal performance was obtained with 0.5 wt% nano-PCM, which resulted in the highest discharge time of 1113 s. The thermal conductivity for this mixture increased from 0.304 to 0.32 W/m.K. Talele et al. [154] investigated the effect of the addition of nano-particles, Al2O3, to the paraffin wax at ambient temperatures of 25 and 35 °C. For the former case, the maximum battery temperature was found to be 43.5 °C at a 2C rate, which increased to about 50.7 °C after increasing the ambient temperature to 35 °C. The battery temperature increases with the increase in ambient temperature due to higher initial thermal load. However, the nano-PCM's superior thermal conductivity promotes faster and more uniform heat transfer throughout the PCM, aiding in temperature regulation under varied conditions. A complete melting process was observed for the nano-PCM as compared to the PCM alone. Subsequently, Zhang et al. [159] also used Al2O3 nano-particles and observed improvement in melting rate and heat dissipation power by 4.7% and 4.9%, respectively. The nano-particles enhance the overall thermal conductivity of the NEPCM, allowing a faster heat transfer rate. This leads to quicker phase transition (melting) and efficient heat distribution, thereby improving PCM's melting dynamics and the heat dissipation ability.

Ren et al. [160] mixed the single-walled carbon nano-particles in 2 and 4 vol% with three types of PCMs, namely, RT27, RT35, and RT58. The simulations were carried out at C rates of 1, 2, and 3. At a higher C rate, the battery performance was mildly affected by the nano-PCMs, RT27 and RT35, and moderately improved by 4.7% for RT58. It was reported that, while nano-PCMs help maintain the uniform temperature distribution and suppress the thermal runaway; the excess weight, increased cost, and the health risk due to the nano-particles cannot be ignored. Shivram and Harish [161] used 2 to 6 vol% single-walled carbon nano-particles to the PCM and observed excellent heat transfer characteristics as compared to the alumina NPs. The nano-PCM with 6 vol% concentration enhanced the heat transfer by 66.5%.

Sazvar and Moqtaderi [162] performed numerical simulations on cooling of Li-ion battery using 5 vol% and 10 vol% Cu nano-particles and fins. The PCM melting rate was found to be increased by 1.8% and 3.9% with the increase in volume fraction of NP by 5% and 10%, respectively. For 10 vol% concentration of NP and fin aspect ratio of 9, the cooling mean power ratio was enhanced by 48%, and the melting rate was improved by 29%. Bais et al. [163] experimentally investigated a novel passive BTMS using the PCM, RT-47 enhanced with γ-Al2O3 nanoparticles at various concentrations (0.5–4 wt%). A 7S2P Li-ion battery pack with different NEPCM configurations was tested at a high 3C discharge rate. Results showed that the 0.5 wt% NEPCM pack achieved the best thermal performance with the lowest maximum temperature (∼42.4 °C) and the longest discharge time (1113 s), outperforming both pure PCM and higher nanoparticle concentrations. Increased thermal conductivity due to nanoparticle addition improved heat rejection; however, excessive nanoparticles reduced latent heat and degraded cooling effectiveness. The mass of all BTMS packs remained under 20% increase versus the bare pack, indicating practical feasibility.

Samykano [164] recommended the use of carbon-based nano-particles for the efficient cooling of the EV batteries. It was suggested to meticulously decide the concentration of the nano-particles due to the trade-off between the performance enhancement and the increase in additional weight of the system. Yao et al. [165] prepared a composite PCM by adding silver nano-particles and expanded graphite to the PCM. The thermal conductivity of the CPCM was about 1.91 W/m.K. For the initial 10 charge- discharge cycles, the maximum battery temperature was limited to 53 °C, and the maximum temperature difference within the battery module was 5 °C. While these additives enhance thermal conductivity and improve heat transfer, their inclusion dilutes the latent heat capacity of the composite, leading to a decrease in the total energy stored or released during melting and solidification cycles. Selvan et al. [166] added silicon-carbide nano-particles to the mixture of lauric acid and stearic acid in different proportions of 0.025, 0.05, and 0.075 vol%. At 0.075 vol%, the agglomeration of particles was observed, which resulted in a reduction in latent heat of the composite PCM. The optimum addition of nano-particles was observed at 0.05 vol%. The addition of nano-particles increased the stability up to 180 °C and improved the thermal conductivity from 0.211 to 0.371 W/m.K.

Despite the improved thermal conductivity offered by NEPCMs, several practical challenges limit their long-term applicability in battery thermal management systems. One of the primary concerns is nanoparticle aggregation and sedimentation during repeated melting–solidification cycles [167,168]. Progressive particle settling can reduce thermal enhancement and create localized hot spots, thereby degrading long-term thermal performance [169–171]. Due to high surface energy and van der Waals forces, nanoparticles tend to cluster over time, leading to non-uniform dispersion and a gradual reduction in effective thermal conductivity.

In addition, increasing nanoparticle concentration often results in higher viscosity of the molten PCM, which suppresses natural convection and offsets the benefits of enhanced conduction [172]. Several investigations have shown that beyond an optimal nanoparticle loading, the thermal performance deteriorates due to agglomeration and phase separation. Surface functionalization of nanoparticles can improve their compatibility with PCMs and enhance dispersion stability by reducing agglomeration and sedimentation during repeated phase-change cycles [173]. Techniques such as surfactant coating or chemical functionalization help maintain uniform particle distribution and consistent thermal performance. In addition, hybrid nanoparticles combining metal oxides and carbon-based materials offer a promising approach to balance improved thermal conductivity with long-term stability. Mechanistically, surface functionalization improves dispersion stability by introducing electrostatic repulsion or steric hindrance between nanoparticles, thereby increasing the energy barrier for aggregation [174,175]. Surfactant-coated nanoparticles generate surface charges that enhance electrostatic stabilization [176], while polymer-grafted or chemically functionalized particles provide steric barriers that limit particle–particle contact during repeated melting–solidification cycles [177,178]. Recent studies have demonstrated that such functionalization strategies can significantly mitigate sedimentation and preserve thermal performance over extended cycling relevant to EV operation. These approaches increase system complexity and cost. Therefore, ensuring stable nanoparticle dispersion and reliable long-term thermo-physical performance remains a critical challenge for the large-scale adoption of nano-PCMs in EV battery thermal management.

Though significant research has been carried out on composite PCMs, the PCM seems to loose and eventually diffuse to the surface [179]. Microencapsulation is an effective method for reducing PCM reaction to the external environment along with regulating volume changes during the phase transition [180]. Encapsulation of PCM is a practical method to contain the liquid phase of PCM and prevent its contamination with the surrounding, which could be hazardous. It may further enhance the thermal and mechanical properties of PCM by increasing the surface area-to-volume ratio and preventing the leakage of the molten PCM [181,182]. To prevent the battery from catching fire, it was recommended to combine micro-encapsulated PCMs (MPCMs) with flame retardants such as red phosphorus, chlorinated paraffins, melamine and melamine cyanurate, boron compounds, etc. These flame retardants suppresses the possibility of thermal runaway occurrence [183]. Beyond intrinsic flame-retardant performance, PCM-based battery thermal management systems must also support compliance with electric-vehicle safety regulations, including requirements in international standards such as ISO 6469 for protection against thermal propagation and fire hazards. While ISO 6469 provides general electrical safety requirements for electric vehicles, compliance with regional safety regulations is equally critical for practical deployment of PCM-based battery thermal management systems. In particular, UN ECE R100 specifies stringent requirements for electric vehicle battery safety, including thermal stability, resistance to fire propagation, electrical isolation and post-crash integrity, whereas such analyses are not widely incorporated in other standards. SAE J2929 aligns with the crash requirements outlined in FMVSS 305:2011 or equivalent regional regulations for front, rear, and side impacts, while ISO 12405-3 mandates adherence to applicable national or regional vehicle crash test regulations. SAE J2464:2009, SAE J2929:2013, and UL 2580:2013 explicitly emphasize the assessment of toxic and flammable emissions and provide corresponding testing procedures, whereas such analyses are not widely incorporated in other standards [184]. Given the critical safety implications, future standardization and regulatory frameworks should adopt harmonized testing guidelines to systematically evaluate chemical hazards associated with automotive batteries, thereby ensuring the safety of vehicle occupants and nearby individuals.

Recent years have seen a lot of interest in the micro-encapsulation of PCMs. Microcapsules are made of an external encasing shell and an internal core substance. Microcapsules are created using a variety of physical and chemical processes [185]. Micro-encapsulation enhances the heat transfer rate of PCMs by increasing the surface area-to-volume ratio. It also prevents leakage of PCM and provides structural stability to the encapsulated PCM. These improvements make MPCMs highly effective for thermal management applications in various fields, including the BTMS. Li et al. [186] used the polymerization method to create phase change materials enclosed in a TiO2/PMMA hybrid shell. The Thermo-gravimetric Analysis (TGA) and Differential Scanning Calorimetry (DSC) results demonstrated that the MPCM is ideal for heat transfer applications due to its excellent thermal stability, high enthalpy, and low degree of supercooling. The thermal conductivity also enhanced from 0.16 to 0.284 W/m.K. The melting enthalpy of the MPCM was 179.9 J/g, and the encapsulation efficiency was 67.2%. This enhances EV battery thermal management by improving thermal stability, reducing supercooling, and ensuring efficient heat absorption and release. This helps prevent overheating of the battery pack and enhances its lifespan and overall performance. Silva et al. [187] studied MPCM by suspension co-polymerization of styrene and methyl methacrylate. The findings showed that microcapsules with properties suitable for industrial applications can be produced with a methyl methacrylate/styrene ratio of 4 and a monomer to paraffin mass ratio of 3. Using styrene and methyl methacrylate improves mechanical strength and thermal conductivity. The optimized monomer ratio ensures effective encapsulation, reducing overheating risk and enhancing battery lifespan. Additionally, the thermal stability of the co-polymer shell ensures long-term reliability without degradation. Merits and demerits of various micro-encapsulation methods are depicted in Table 4 .

Aiswarya et al. [212] used the in-situ polymerization method to form a novel MPCM, which has a paraffin core, a melamine formaldehyde shell, and a hybrid thermal conductivity enhancer interface made of graphene oxide (GO) and magnesium oxide (MgO). The modified MPCMs attained the encapsulation rate of 88%. This reflects a highly effective shell-core structure, which is essential for preserving the integrity of the paraffin and preventing leakage during phase transitions. This led to an increase in thermal stability by 50 °C to 70 °C, latent heat of 177.8 J/g, thermal conductivity from 0.32 to 0.44 W/m.K, and drastic reduction in PCM leakage by 98.6%. Tang et al. [213] introduced a novel hybrid cooling method that combines MPCM and MPCM suspension for the cooling of a battery pack. The MPCMs were prepared with a dual-core made of octadecane and docosane and a melamine-urea-formaldehyde shell. The MPCMs include nitrogen-doped multiwalled carbon nanotubes (N-MWCNT) to improve thermal conductivity. The best performance was obtained for octadecane/docosane ratio of 1:2 and 10 wt% N-MWCNT, yielding a latent heat of 100.8 J/g and thermal conductivity of 0.88 W/m.K. The combination helps extend the temperature modulation range and melting peak width, improving temperature regulation in the battery system. At a 3C discharge rate, the battery temperature was maintained well below 49 °C, with a maximum temperature difference within the battery pack lower than 2.5 °C.

Another efficient way of improving the thermal conductivity of the bulk PCM is to insert the metal fins. The fins are commonly used to augment the heat transfer rate due to their simple structure, easy manufacturing, availability in bulk, and cost-effectiveness [214,215]. Enormous studies have been performed to assess the effect of different fin structures on the thermal performance of a PCM-assisted battery cooling system. Sun et al. [216] reported that the longitudinal fins and spherical rings help improve the heat transfer rate of a PCM-based BTMS by significantly increasing the effective surface area and creating conductive pathways within the low thermal conductivity PCM. The metallic fins act as thermal bridges, rapidly conducting heat from the battery surface to the PCM, where it is absorbed during phase change. This enhanced heat dissipation mechanism lowers the rate of temperature rise and maintains more uniform temperature distribution across the battery pack. The operational life of this system was extended by 220% compared to the pure PCM-based BTMS. It was also observed that the combination of PCM and fins substantially lowers the rate of temperature rise. Choudhari et al. [217] studied the effect of various fin geometries such as rectangular, triangular, trapezoidal, I-shape, and T-shape. The best and the worst thermal performance was observed with I-shaped and triangular fins, respectively. The increase in the number of fins may not be advantageous as it reduces the amount of PCM and, hence, the heat storage capability. The optimized combination of PCM and fins led to the reduction in battery temperature by 2.4% and 9.3% as compared to the PCM without fins at 2C and 3C current rates, respectively. Weng et al. [218] conducted experiments to examine the impact of longitudinal and circular fins on the PCM-based BTMS. The best thermal performance was observed with four longitudinal fins, with a maximum reduction in battery temperature by 2.7 °C. Compared to the longitudinal fins model, the combination of PCM with longitudinal fins reduced the battery temperature by 5.5. Shojaeefard et al. [219] computationally investigated the effect of fins and their alignment on the PCM based thermal management system for a battery module. The fins were aligned at different angles of 53°, 37°, 0°, −37°, and −53°. The best cooling performance was observed with the horizontal fins, leading to the largest melt fraction of the PCM. The fins, which were slightly aligned towards the battery cell, break into three parts of the PCM, whereas, the fins aligned substantially away from the cell surface, represented breaking of the PCM into more than three parts. The latter indicates substantial improvement in the melt fraction of the PCM.

In hot climate regions, the operating temperature substantially hampers the capacity and lifespan of the Li-ion battery module. Ping et al. [220] studied the influence of PCM-cum-fin structure to control the battery temperature at high ambient temperature conditions of 40 °C. The reduction in fin thickness decreases the maximum battery temperature due to the increase in PCM-fin surface contact area. By reducing the fin spacing, the number of fins can be increased, leading to a higher heat transfer area. However, this reduces the PCM volume and hence negatively influences the overall performance of the cooling system. The optimized PCM-fin structure successfully kept the battery surface temperature below 51 °C at a high discharge rate of 3C. It improved the PCM melt fraction by 29%. Zhao et al. [221] performed experiments and simulations to cool the Li-ion battery module at a high discharge rate of 5C using the cold plate with uniform and non-uniform diameter pin fins. The performance was assessed using the four parameters, namely, maximum battery temperature, temperature standard deviation, pressure drop, and cooling system weight. The uniform diameter fins reduce the first two parameters however increase the last two. The non-uniform diameter pin fins demonstrated better performance than the uniform ones in terms of reduction in power consumption, cooling system weight, and the temperature standard deviation by 29.8%, 29%, and 17.4%, respectively.

Ajay Kumar et al. [222] used multiple arrays of triangular and elliptical-shaped fins along the height of a single Li-ion cell. The annulus space was filled with a PCM, OM42. At 70 W power input, the maximum extension in cell operation for the triangular and elliptical fins were 10% and 20%, respectively. After the inclusion of the PCM, these extensions were 250% for the PCM without any fins, 270% for PCM with triangular fins, and 280% for PCM with elliptical fins. The better performance with the elliptical fins was attributed to 37% additional surface area over the triangular fins. This additional surface area helps increase the heat transfer rate. Lyu et al. [223] experimentally evaluated PCM-based heat sinks with three configurations: conventional aluminum fins, heat-pipe fins, and heat-pipe fins combined with copper foam. The hybrid configuration incorporating heat-pipe fins and copper foam significantly improved thermal performance, reducing the maximum internal temperature difference by up to 25% and suppressing base temperature rise effectively. Heat-pipe fins facilitated vertical heat conduction to upper PCM layers, while copper foam provided multidirectional conduction pathways promoting uniform melting and solidification. This synergy extended the thermal buffering time during both pre-sensible heating and melting stages, overcoming the intrinsic low thermal conductivity of PCMs and enhancing temperature uniformity while minimizing local overheating.

Liu et al. [224] designed a vapor chamber paired with a fin structure-based BTMS for thermal management of a Li-ion battery pack at different discharge rates of 2C, 3C, and 5C. The use of a vapor chamber with arc-type fins not only reduced the maximum battery temperature but also maintained the uniform temperature distribution within the battery pack. Wang et al. [225] compared the performance of graphite sheet fins with aluminum alloy fins for the BTMS application. The graphite fins outperformed the aluminum ones in terms of maximum battery temperature reduction and uniformity in battery surface temperature. This is attributed to the higher thermal conductivity of the graphite sheets in the x-direction. Near the surface edge, the battery surface temperature was higher for the graphite sheet as compared to the aluminum sheet. This was due to the lower thermal conductivity of the graphite sheet (15 W/m.K) in the Y-direction.

The heat sink is a passive cooling system that transfers heat away from the battery via convection and radiation. It is often constructed of a material with high thermal conductivity, such as copper or aluminum. It has multiple fins that increase the surface area for heat dissipation [226]. The heat sink dissipates heat from the battery surface through natural / forced convection to the surrounding, keeping the battery at a safe operating temperature. The heat sink, combined with a fan or a liquid cooling system, further enhances the battery cooling performance [227].

Nagmule and Salunkhe [228] cooled a 3 × 3 battery module using the PCM and a heat sink. The heat sink had 12 fins, each with 3 mm thickness and a 4 mm gap between the two successive fins. For 10, 20, 30, and 40 W power inputs, the combination of PCM and heat sink enhanced the battery module operation by 100.8, 35.5, 24.7, and 21.2%, respectively. At high power inputs of 30 and 40 W, the thermal management of the central cell was found to be challenging. Akula et al. [229] investigated the use of pin fins for thermal management of lithium-ion batteries. Numerical simulations were conducted to investigate the temperature regulation capabilities of paraffin-based heat sinks with plate fins and pin fins. The pin fins performed better than the plate fins in lowering the average battery surface temperature by 3.3 °C at 5C discharge rate. A thorough numerical analysis was carried out by varying the pin fin size, number of fins, heat loads, PCMs, and ambient conditions. Among all configurations studied, the heat sink with 260 pin fins, each of 1 mm size, turned out to be the most efficient configuration.

Soliman et al. [230] introduced a novel heat sink design for lithium-ion battery thermal management, combining hybrid air convection and fins internally filled with multiple PCMs (RT-35, RT-31). The optimized configuration of four fins with dual PCMs and constant total PCM volume significantly lowered battery temperatures, by up to 54.5% at a 10C discharge rate compared to a conventional smooth PCM enclosure. Unlike traditional setups that exhibited substantial top-bottom thermal non-uniformity (up to 16.3 °C), the new design maintained temperature differences within 1 °C, demonstrating greatly improved thermal uniformity, cooling effectiveness, and potential battery lifespan even across high discharge rates and elevated ambient temperatures. Alzwayi et al. [231] tested the performance of a Li-ion cell with longitudinal and spiral fins on the cell surface. At different current rates, the effects of fin number, rotation, thickness, length, and position were investigated. Results showed that the fins can significantly lower the maximum cell temperature, particularly at low Reynolds numbers. Having more than three fins resulted in a flow barrier surrounding the cell, which increases the battery temperature even after the increase in heat transfer area. Heat transfer between the cell and air cooling is greatly impacted by the fins' orientation. A half-length longitudinal fin, as opposed to a full-length fin, raises the cell temperature by 1.5 °C. The spiral fins reduced the material usage by 65.6% and decreased the cell temperature by 3.2% as compared to the longitudinal fins. Zare et al. [232] investigated the effect of fin quantity on PCM melting time, energy density, heat storage capacity, and thermal management performance. Four internal and external fins were attached to the cell surface. As compared to PCM cooling without fins, the PCM with fins reduced the cell surface temperature by 9.9 K at 3C discharge rate and by 17.5 K at 5C discharge rate. Even at a higher ambient temperature of 308 K, the maximum battery surface temperature was maintained below 318 K, which falls in the best operating temperature range for the Li-ion batteries. The incorporation of internal and external fins increased the energy density by 10% and heat storage capacity by 11% with a higher fin efficiency of 32.8%.

Heat pipe is a passive device that facilitates efficient heat transfer by leveraging phase change and capillary action. Heat pipes are widely used in electronics cooling, HVAC systems, spacecraft thermal control, and energy systems due to their high thermal conductivity, reliability, and passive operation. Fig. 7 shows a schematic representation of the working principle of a heat pipe. It consists of a vacuum-sealed tube, wick structure, and working fluid. The heat pipe is divided into different sections, namely, evaporator, condenser, and an optional adiabatic section. The evaporative section of the heat pipe is kept in contact with the heat source, the battery pack in the present case. The working fluid inside the heat pipe receives this heat through conduction and transforms from liquid to the vapor phase at substantially lower temperatures [233].

The vapor moves from the evaporator section to the condenser section, where it loses thermal energy to the environment resulting in phase change from the vapor to the liquid state. The working fluid in liquid state returns to the evaporator section through the capillary action and the cycle repeats till the system and the surrounding attains thermal equilibrium. One of the major advantages of the heat pipe is the significantly increased heat transfer rate that is several times higher than the traditional heat transfer augmentation methods [234]. The heat pipe technology has been extensively studied for BTMS application [235,236]. Practical heat pipe applications require working fluids that can undergo efficient evaporation and condensation within the operating temperature range while remaining chemically compatible with the heat pipe materials. The selection of a working fluid is primarily governed by its thermophysical properties, which directly affect heat transfer performance and compatibility with the casing and wick material of the heat pipe. Since lithium-ion batteries operate optimally within a temperature range of 20–40 °C, low-temperature heat pipes employing water, acetone, or ethanol as working fluids are considered most suitable for battery thermal management applications [237]. Table 5 summarizes properties of the most commonly used working fluids in heat pipe.

The choice of a suitable working fluid for the heat pipe is a crucial factor in determining thermal performance under various battery operating conditions. Water-based heat pipes are widely preferred for lithium-ion battery thermal management due to their high latent heat of vaporization and superior thermal conductivity, making them particularly effective under moderate operating conditions and sustained discharge rates [242]. However, during fast charging or highly transient thermal loads, low-boiling-point fluids such as acetone can offer faster start-up and improved response, enabling rapid heat removal during short-duration thermal spikes [239]. These performance benefits are accompanied by important trade-offs. Water requires careful control of corrosion, especially in copper‑aluminum hybrid assemblies, while acetone raises compatibility concerns with elastomeric seals and poses higher flammability risk. Therefore, selection of the working fluid should consider not only thermal performance but also material compatibility, safety, and long-term reliability within the EV environment.

Kim et al. [52] demonstrated that at high discharge rates, heat pipes significantly reduce cell-to-ambient temperature deviation to less than 3 °C, outperforming active air cooling due to efficient latent heat transfer. Numerical analysis verified that a thermal resistance of about 1.25–1.65 K/W is suitable for copper heat pipes, confirming their effectiveness under varied C-rates. It was concluded that such passive systems provide a low-cost, noise-free, and power-efficient alternative for battery thermal management, especially under high-load conditions. Gao et al. [243] developed a hybrid BTMS consisting of PCM and heat pipes. At 2C, 3C and 4C rates, the maximum reduction in battery temperature was 25.7%, 23.3% and 34.9%, respectively as compared to the cooling system with only the PCM. Additionally, the temperature uniformity was substantially improved, with values lower than 5 °C. It was recommended to use the hybrid system for the battery cooling operation. Jang et al. [244] compared the performance of aluminum plate and heat pipe for the cooling of a pouch-type Li-ion battery. The reduction in average battery temperature was 22.7% and 32% for aluminum plate and heat pipe, respectively, over the natural convection cooling. The heat pipe also outperformed the aluminum plate in terms of maintaining a uniform temperature distribution within the battery. Subsequent experiments were conducted by varying the heat ratio non-uniformly from 1 to 3. In these conditions, the heat pipe performance was improved by 41% over the aluminum plate.
