The maximum temperature difference within the battery was 7.6 °C and 4.4 °C for aluminum plate and heat pipe, respectively. Zhou et al. [245] used hybrid oscillating heat pipe (OHP) charged with ethanol-water mixture with a volume ratio of 1:1. The carbon nanotubes (CNT) were added to this working fluid in different mass concentrations of 0.05 wt% to 0.5 wt%. As compared to the water-ethanol mixture, the combination of water-ethanol and CNT reduced the battery evaporator temperature and thermal resistance by 9.8 °C and 0.278 °C/W, respectively. This combination kept the average battery temperature lower than 45 °C with a maximum temperature difference of less than 1 °C.

Hu et al. [246] investigated effect of PCM and flat heat pipe on the thermal performance of a Li-ion battery pack. At a discharge rate of 3C, the maximum battery temperature was limited to 318.6 K for the heat pipe-based cooling and 312.3 K for the cooling system consisting of PCM and heat pipe. For the latter case, the maximum temperature difference within the battery was only 2.65 K, which also indicates uniform electrochemical behavior, reducing the risk of thermal runaway. Bernagozzi et al. [247] employed BTMS with a flat heat pipe and graphite sheets at the base of a prismatic cell module. These results were later compared with those of the cold plate. The battery cell was sandwiched between the 1 mm thick graphite sheets. A flat plate copper heat pipe was used with two different working fluids, water and ethanol. With the cold plate cooling, the maximum battery temperature reached up to 35.1 °C, whereas, after using the heat pipe with graphite sheets, it reduced to 31.5 °C. The latter could maintain the uniform temperature distribution within the cell with a maximum variation of 2 °C. Subsequently, Smith et al. [248] recommended the use of heat pipes and cold plates to control the temperature of high power EV batteries. A total of 36 copper heat pipes with water as a working fluid were used to extract the heat load of 400 W per module. The heat pipes with a cold plate cooling system could maintain the battery module temperature up to 55 °C and a maximum temperature difference of less than 5 °C. This two-stage cooling system prevents thermal buildup, which is essential for high heat flux scenarios like fast charging or aggressive discharging.

Zhang et al. [249] performed experimental investigations on heat pipe along with the porous metal foam impregnated with the PCM. The heat pipe transported heat from the cell to the metal foam, which was not in direct contact with the battery module. The experiments were conducted at different discharge rates of 1C, 3C, 4C, and 5C. Up to a 4C discharge rate, the maximum battery temperature was limited to 45 °C with the proposed cooling system. Even at a high discharge rate of 5C, the maximum temperature difference was less than 5 °C. Alihosseini et al. [250] controlled the battery pack temperature using a custom-made heat pipe at various ambient temperatures in an environmental test chamber. The experimental results showed that while battery surface temperature increased with ambient temperature, the heat pipe effectively moderated this rise and uniformly maintained the battery surface temperature below 40 °C. In the absence of the heat pipe, the battery temperature reached up to 45 °C after ten cycles. This demonstrates the significance of the heat pipe in damping out the temperature fluctuations and maintaining uniform temperature distribution within a battery pack.

Behi et al. [251] reported that a single heat pipe could provide up to 29.1% of the required cooling load at an 8C discharge rate. At the module level, the liquid cooling system and the heat pipes outperformed the natural air cooling, reducing the module temperature by 29.9% and 32.6%, respectively. The enhanced cooling performance of heat pipes and liquid cooling systems is primarily due to their superior heat transfer mechanism compared to natural air cooling. Heat pipes efficiently transfer heat via phase change and capillary action, enabling targeted cooling at critical hotspots. Wei et al. [252] experimentally tested the oscillating heat pipes with tube condensers and flat-plate evaporators to cool the battery pack. Different working fluids, namely, pure water, ethanol, and binary fluid mixtures with mixing ratios of 1:1, 2:1, 3:1, and 4:1 and volumetric filling ratios of 30%, 40%, and 50%, were used. Among the above, the heat pipe with a water-ethanol mixing ratio of 1:1 and a filling ratio of 30% led to the highest heat transfer rate. For 56 W power input, the average battery pack temperature was maintained below 46.5 °C with a maximum temperature variation of 1–2 °C within the pack.

Chavan et al. [253] explored the use of heat pipes to enhance the heat transfer from batteries to cold plates. The findings demonstrated that heat pipes efficiently enabled heat transfer and preserved temperature uniformity along the battery length. The maximum battery temperature without the BTMS was 44 °C, which reduced to 38 °C at a 1C discharge rate, after using the heat pipes. The maximum temperature variation was found to be 2.5 °C compared to the cold plates cooling system. Gao et al. [243] analyzed the thermal performance of a battery module with PCM and heat pipe cooling system. At 2C, 3C, and 4C discharge rates, the combined PCM and heat pipe cooling system led to the maximum reduction in battery surface temperature by 25.7%, 23.2%, and 34.9%, respectively, as compared to the PCM alone. For the PCM alone case, the maximum temperature difference was observed to be 9.8 °C, which was further reduced to less than 5 °C for the combined PCM and heat pipe cooling. Wang et el. [53] proposed a novel BTMS that uses heat pipe, an aluminum fin, PCM, and a liquid cooling tube. The findings revealed that with a 2C discharge rate and ambient temperature of 30 °C, the maximum temperature of the battery reached up to 45.9 °C with a maximum temperature difference of 2 °C. This narrow temperature difference is critical for maintaining cell-to-cell temperature uniformity, which directly impacts battery performance, safety, and lifespan. The incorporation of PCM significantly reduced the temperature rise rate due to its heat absorption capability, acting as a thermal buffer during peak loads or transient heating phases. This behavior not only protects the battery from thermal spikes but also reduces the burden on the cooling elements, improving overall system efficiency. Table 6 shows a summary of various heat pipes used in BTMS.

Padalkar et al. [254] investigated an innovative heat pipe–based battery thermal management system (BTMS) for cylindrical lithium-ion packs, featuring a circumferential cell contactor and heat sink optimized for both natural and forced convection. Experimental and simulation results showed that, compared to no cooling, the novel system could reduce cell temperatures by up to 46.6% (natural convection) and 62.3% (forced convection) at a 3C discharge, maintaining safe operation below 52 °C and minimal peak temperature variation (ΔTmax ≈ 3 °C). The high thermal conductivity and rapid heat transport of heat pipes allow quick absorption and transfer of heat away from battery cell. The design ensures uniform heat dissipation by efficiently conducting heat from battery surfaces to heat sinks, enabling scalable, compact, and energy-efficient BTMS for high-rate cylindrical battery packs.

Lei et al. [261] performed parametric optimization of spray cooling for the condenser section of a heat pipe. The spray frequency and duty cycle found to have a mild effect on the cooling performance of the heat pipe. The proposed cooling system could reduce the maximum temperature of the battery pack by 29.2 °C at a high discharge current of 24 A. After dropping the battery temperature below 40 °C, the spray cooling was replaced by forced air cooling. This strategy reflects a well-designed energy management approach, where the more energy-intensive spray cooling is used only during peak thermal loads, and lower-power air cooling takes over during low-load phases. This hybrid control method not only conserves energy but also prevents overcooling and condensation issues that can arise from continuous spray application. Leng et al. [262] performed energy saving assessment for Li-ion battery pack using the PCM and heat pipes. It was reported that the heat pipes work more efficiently with the cooling fans on the condenser section, though it reduces the energy saving. The highest energy saving of the combined active and passive cooling system was around 81.8%. Peng et al. [263] used a PCM-heat pipe combination for thermal management of the Li-ion battery module. Acetone was used as a working fluid for the heat pipe and circular fins were attached on the condenser section of the heat pipe. Numerical simulations were carried out at different discharge rates of 0.5, 1, and 2C. At 2C discharge rate and ambient temperature of 25 °C, the battery module reached up to 47.7 °C. The battery thermal performance was substantially improved by operating at lower C-rates and provided higher recovery time for the charge-discharge cycles.

Behi et al. [264] analyzed lithium-titanate battery cell performance with natural convection, heat pipes, and the combination of forced convection for the cell and the heat pipes. The forced air cooling was used to cool the condenser section of the heat pipe. The reduction in cell temperature was found to be 13.7%, 31.6%, and 33.4% for natural convection, heat pipes with forced convection, and a combination of forced cooling for the cell and heat pipes, respectively. Subsequent experiments were carried out at an 8C discharge rate and various ambient temperatures of 10, 22, and 35 °C. The corresponding cell temperatures for the forced convection cooling were 27.1, 39.6, and 52.5 °C, respectively. Su et al. [265] developed a novel thin heat pipe array based cooling system for prismatic lithium-ion battery packs. It demonstrated excellent heat dissipation capacity and temperature uniformity under various heat generation rates and airflow conditions. Experimental results showed that the device significantly reduced maximum battery temperatures below 50 °C and maintained temperature differences within 5 °C during high current operations. Horizontal placement of the heat pipe array enhanced cooling performance and response time compared to vertical placement due to improved convective heat transfer area and less thermal buoyancy i.e. buoyancy-driven fluid motion caused by temperature-induced density gradients interference. The total thermal resistance decreased with increasing airflow, enhancing heat dissipation, though performance limits were observed at very high heat generation rates due to partial dry-out of the heat pipes. This reliable and efficient thermal management solution supports longer battery lifespan, safety, and scalability for energy storage applications.

Liang et al. [266] used six inclined U-shaped micro-heat pipes to cool the Li-ion battery module. These flat and compact heat pipes significantly increased the equivalent thermal conductivity to 4350 W/m.K. The maximum reduction in cell surface temperature was 16% and improvement in temperature uniformity was 60% over the baseline case. The significant reduction in surface temperature and improved uniformity are attributed to the high thermal conductivity of the inclined U-shaped micro-heat pipes, which efficiently spread heat across the battery module. The inclined orientation enhances phase change heat transfer by promoting better liquid flow and contact with heat sources, leading to uniform heat distribution. Kleiner et al. [267] used the heat pipe based cooling technique for the fast charging of Li-ion battery. Two heat pipes were attached to each prismatic cell. The maximum reduction in jelly roll temperature was 4.3 °C. The jelly roll temperature non-uniformity was also found to be significantly reduced after using the heat pipes which improved the lifespan of the battery pack. To provide an overall synthesis of literature covering diverse passive cooling strategies, Table 7 consolidates the comparative data on PCM, NEPCM, and heat pipe-based hybrid configurations.

Table 7 highlights substantial methodological variability across reported studies, including different discharge rates (0.5C–10C), ambient conditions, and battery configurations, which limits direct quantitative comparison. While nano-enhanced and composite PCMs consistently improve effective thermal conductivity, this enhancement is frequently accompanied by partial latent heat degradation, indicating a clear trade-off between heat transfer rate and thermal storage capacity. Studies conducted at low-to-moderate C-rates often report favorable temperature reduction, whereas high-C-rate operation (>4C) exposes the limitations of standalone PCM systems and underscores the superior reliability of hybrid configurations such as PCM–heat pipe and fin-assisted systems. These disparities emphasize the need for discharge-rate-specific benchmarking while evaluating passive battery thermal management strategies.

Carbon-based additives such as graphene, expanded graphite, and CNTs provide substantial thermal conductivity enhancement, often exceeding one order of magnitude, but at the cost of significant latent heat reduction, typically in the range of 18–43%. In contrast, oxide-based nanoparticles (e.g., Al₂O₃, CuO) offer moderate conductivity enhancement (≈50–85%) with comparatively lower latent heat penalties (<10–15%). These quantitative trends demonstrate a clear trade-off between heat transfer capability and energy storage capacity, highlighting the existence of an optimal filler loading beyond which further conductivity gains yield diminishing thermal benefits.

From an economic perspective, the large-scale adoption of BTMS must balance thermal benefits against material and manufacturing costs. The BTMS configurations that use graphene-enhanced PCMs or PCM–heat-pipe combinations can achieve superior cooling, however high material and processing costs make their deployment challenging compared with simple aluminum fins and heat sinks. Air-cooled BTMS is the most cost-effective solution, with system costs of $1500–3000 and annual operating costs of $100–300. However, this cooling system offers limited cooling effect. Liquid-cooled systems deliver superior cooling performance at higher costs ($3000–6000 system cost and $200–500 per year operating cost) and greater complexity. Hybrid cooling techniques provide a balanced compromise, achieving improved thermal performance and energy efficiency at moderate costs ($2500–5000 system cost and $150–400 per year operating cost), making them suitable for safety and efficiency-driven applications [269].

To enhance the integrative understanding of various passive cooling techniques, a performance radar chart is introduced. This visual comparison reveals that pure PCM based cooling systems exhibit excellent energy storage with limited thermal conductivity, whereas composite and nano-PCM configurations achieve a more balanced thermal and economic performance. Heat pipes demonstrate superior thermal conductivity and temperature uniformity but lack energy storage capability. The PCM-heat pipe hybrid system achieves the most comprehensive performance profile, with exceptional cooling effectiveness and temperature uniformity, though at higher cost. Fig. 8 presents a multi-dimensional performance comparison of passive cooling technologies using a radar chart representation.

The present paper explores the feasibility of using various passive thermal management strategies for lithium-ion batteries, which include phase change materials with thermal conductivity enhancers and heat pipe cooling. During the battery operation, an efficient battery thermal management system shall control the maximum battery temperature and maintain uniform temperature distribution with a maximum temperature difference of 5 °C within the battery pack. The important findings of the present work are listed below.
•
At high current rates (≥4C), PCM–heat pipe cooling systems demonstrate superior thermal performance, effectively controlling peak battery temperature and maintaining temperature uniformity (ΔT < 5 °C), making them the most reliable passive solution for high-power EV applications.
•
Heat pipes provide rapid and efficient heat transport due to their low thermal resistance, enabling improved temperature control and uniformity; copper and aluminum heat pipes using water or ethanol are most suitable for battery operating temperature ranges.
•
PCMs are promising passive cooling media for controlling temperature rise in battery packs; however, they must be coupled with secondary cooling to remove stored latent heat and avoid progressive temperature build-up over long duty cycles.
•
The incorporation of additives to PCM, such as nano-particles, graphene, expanded graphite, etc., improves the thermal conductivity of the composite PCM. However, it deteriorates the thermophysical properties of the composite PCM, especially the latent heat and melting point. This gives a trade-off between the heat transfer rate and the energy storage capacity. The reduction in natural convection heat transfer rate during the molten state of the nano-PCM is of primary concern.
•
A combination of PCM with fins outperforms the fins alone. The increase in number of fins reduces the PCM volume and negatively affects the cooling performance though it increases the thermal conductivity. Increase in cooling system weight is a major concern with the use of fins and heat sink.
•
From economics point of view, the air-cooled system is the most cost-effective cooling system followed by hybrid cooling system and finally the liquid cooling system.

Future research on passive cooling techniques for Li-ion batteries should adhere to a systematic and prioritized approach encompassing material-level innovation, system-level optimization, intelligent modeling, and sustainability assessment.

Material-level innovation:
•
Future research should focus on developing multi-scale composite systems incorporating graphene, metal foams, or CNTs to achieve balanced thermal conductivity and latent storage performance.
•
Enhancing dispersion stability through surface-functionalized nanoparticles and optimized sonication parameters.
•
Conducting long-term thermal cycling (>1000 cycles) and recyclability assessments to evaluate durability and environmental safety.
Such material innovations will mitigate the conductivity, latent heat trade-off and form the foundation for next-generation passive BTMS.

System-level optimization
•
Next stage research should focus on compact, high-performance hybrid architectures combining PCM–heat pipe or PCM–microchannel systems for improved temperature uniformity (ΔT < 5 °C) at high C-rates.
•
Developing lightweight, modular pack designs to enhance scalability and manufacturability.
•
Employ coupled CFD electrochemical modeling with experimental validation to optimize transient thermal behavior and geometry.

Intelligent modeling
•
Future research should emphasize the adoption of AI and machine learning models for thermal prediction, energy-efficient control, and failure diagnostics under real-time operating conditions.

Sustainability assessment
•
Upcoming research should prioritize sustainable PCMs sourced from bio-based or low-carbon materials, with lifecycle assessments to evaluate energy use and carbon footprint. Aligning developments with UN SDGs 7, 9, and 13 will promote cleaner, safer, and more energy-efficient battery technologies for a sustainable future.

Based on the past literature, the following future directions are formulated as specific hypotheses and benchmark protocols to enable standardized, testable evaluation of nano-PCMs and passive BTMS architectures.

Hypothesis: For a standard cylindrical lithium-ion battery module operating at discharge rates between 1C and 4C, NEPCMs containing 1–5 wt% high-thermal-conductivity additives (e.g., graphene, Al₂O₃, CNTs) will reduce the peak cell temperature by ≥10 °C and maintain maximum temperature non-uniformity (ΔT) below 5 °C, while limiting latent heat reduction to ≤10%, compared with a baseline PCM under identical operating conditions.

Benchmark protocol: All nano-PCMs should be evaluated using a reference battery module with fixed geometry, electrical configuration, ambient temperature, and cooling boundary conditions. Thermal performance should be assessed through surface and inter-cell temperature measurements under controlled C-rate operation, while thermophysical properties should be characterized using standardized DSC and thermal conductivity measurements after 0, 100, 500, and 1000 thermal or charge–discharge cycles. This structured and repeatable testing framework enables rigorous cross-study comparison and supports the identification of scalable and durable passive cooling strategies.

Future research should include mechanistic and system-level validation of hybrid cooling architectures that integrate nano-PCMs with heat pipes in addition to material-level benchmarking. A key hypothesis is that the synergistic coupling of latent heat absorption by PCM and rapid heat transport by heat pipes may suppress localized hot spots and delay PCM saturation under transient operating conditions. Mechanistic investigations should focus on interfacial thermal resistance at the PCM–heat pipe interface, heat pipe activation thresholds, and the evolution of PCM melting–solidification fronts during dynamic loading. To enable empirical validation, hybrid PCM–heat pipe modules should be evaluated using real-time temperature monitoring through embedded thermocouples or fiber-optic sensors integrated with battery management system (BMS) data. Validation protocols should include variable C-rate profiles and representative driving cycles to capture realistic thermal transients. Future work should employ AI- and machine-learning-based predictive models to optimize CPCM and NEPCM formulations by correlating the type of additive, its concentration, and morphology with thermal conductivity enhancement and latent heat retention.

G. Shruti: Writing – original draft, Data curation. Pramod B. Salunkhe: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Conceptualization. B. Satish Shenoy: Writing – review & editing, Supervision.
